questions.text: What is the primary function of a Geographic Information System (GIS)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Store textual or numeric information only

choices.label: C
choices.text: Store and analyze maps, weather data, and satellite images

choices.label: D
choices.text: Extract business information from very large databases

questions.explanation: GIS are specialized databases designed to handle spatial data such as maps and satellite imagery.

questions.text: The term 'Miniworld' or 'Universe of Discourse' (UoD) refers to:

choices.label: B
choices.text: The specific aspect of the real world that a database represents

choices.label: C
choices.text: The meta-data stored in a database catalog

choices.label: D
choices.text: A database containing only multimedia files

questions.explanation: A database represents some specific aspect of the real world, called the miniworld or UoD.

questions.text: Which of the following defines a Database Management System (DBMS)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A collection of related data with implicit meaning

choices.label: B
choices.text: A specific piece of hardware used for storage

choices.label: C
choices.text: A collection of programs that enables users to create and maintain a database

choices.label: D
choices.text: The set of records stored in a file system

questions.explanation: A DBMS is the software package that facilitates the creation and maintenance of a computerized database.

questions.text: In the context of database management, what does 'Defining' a database involve?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Specifying data types, structures, and constraints

questions.explanation: Defining involves specifying the structure, types, and constraints of the data to be stored.

questions.text: What is 'Meta-data' in a database system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The raw data values for students and courses

choices.label: B
choices.text: The hardware configuration of the database server

choices.label: C
choices.text: Descriptive information about the database structure stored in a catalog

choices.label: D
choices.text: The log of user login attempts

questions.explanation: Meta-data is descriptive information (database definition) stored in the catalog or dictionary.

questions.text: Which property ensures that either all operations in a transaction are executed or none are?
questions.explanation: Atomicity ensures that a transaction is treated as a single unit; it either completes entirely or fails entirely.

questions.text:
Consider the following database system components. Match them to the correct hierarchy level:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[End Users] --> B[DBMS Software]
    B --> C[Stored Database Definition / Meta-data]
    B --> D[Stored Database]
```
What is the name of the 'Stored Database Definition' used by the DBMS software to achieve self-description?
questions.explanation: The database catalog stores the meta-data, allowing the system to be self-describing.

questions.text: Which phase of database design focuses on specifying the data types and structures for the UNIVERSITY database?
questions.explanation: Logical design (or Data Model Mapping) involves mapping the conceptual schema into the data model of the specific DBMS (like relational structures).

questions.text: What is the main drawback of traditional file processing compared to the database approach?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Data is defined once and accessed by many

choices.label: C
choices.text: Each user defines and implements the specific files they need, leading to redundancy

choices.label: D
choices.text: It uses meta-data which slows down the system

questions.explanation: Traditional file processing leads to data redundancy and inconsistency because each application maintains its own files.

questions.text: Program-data independence is achieved because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Data is stored on separate physical disks

choices.label: B
choices.text: The structure of data files is stored in the DBMS catalog separately from access programs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Users are not allowed to access the data directly

choices.label: D
choices.text: The DBMS uses C++ to store objects

questions.explanation: Storing the structure in the catalog (meta-data) allows the DBMS to change storage structures without modifying application programs.

questions.text: Which characteristic of the database approach allows a student to see their grades while a registrar sees student billing information from the same database?
questions.explanation: Multiple views allow different users to see different subsets or perspectives of the database.

questions.text: What does the 'Isolation' property of a transaction ensure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A transaction must be completed in its entirety

choices.label: B
choices.text: Each transaction appears to execute without interference from other concurrent transactions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only one user can use the database at a time

choices.label: D
choices.text: The database is protected from hardware failure

questions.explanation: Isolation ensures that the execution of one transaction is not affected by others occurring at the same time.

questions.text: Who is responsible for authorizing access to the database and coordinating/monitoring its use?
questions.explanation: The DBA is responsible for administration tasks like security, authorization, and resource acquisition.

questions.text: Users who use 'canned transactions' to interact with the database (e.g., bank tellers) are classified as:
questions.explanation: Naive or parametric users interact through standard, predefined transactions.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered a 'Worker behind the scene'?
questions.explanation: Workers behind the scene develop the tools and the DBMS software itself, rather than using the database content.

questions.text: What is the purpose of 'Controlled Redundancy' in a database?
questions.explanation: While redundancy is generally avoided, controlled redundancy (denormalization) is sometimes used to speed up specific queries.

questions.text: A 'Referential Integrity' constraint ensures that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Every record has a unique ID

choices.label: B
choices.text: A record in one file (e.g., Grade) must relate to an existing record in another file (e.g., Student)

choices.label: C
choices.text: The database is backed up every day

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only authorized users can access the data

questions.explanation: Referential integrity maintains consistency between related files.

questions.text: What is the 'Impedance Mismatch' problem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The difference between disk speed and CPU speed

choices.label: B
choices.text: The mismatch between data structures in a programming language (like C++) and those in a database model

choices.label: C
choices.text: The conflict between two users updating the same record

choices.label: D
choices.text: The failure of a transaction to achieve atomicity

questions.explanation: Impedance mismatch refers to the difficulty of mapping object-oriented programming structures to relational database structures.

questions.text: Under which condition is it better to use regular files instead of a DBMS?

choices.label: A
choices.text: When the database is expected to grow rapidly

choices.label: B
choices.text: When there are multiple users accessing the data

choices.label: C
choices.text: When there are stringent real-time requirements that DBMS overhead might hinder

choices.label: D
choices.text: When complex relationships need to be represented

questions.explanation: DBMS overhead (security, concurrency, etc.) can be too high for certain time-critical or storage-limited applications.

questions.text: Which of these is an example of an 'Update' operation in a UNIVERSITY database?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Retrieve the transcript of a student

choices.label: B
choices.text: List the prerequisites of a course

choices.label: C
choices.text: Changing a student's class from 'freshman' to 'sophomore'

choices.label: D
choices.text: Generating a list of students in the Fall section

questions.explanation: Changing data values constitutes an update, whereas simply listing or retrieving data are queries.

questions.text: Which property of database systems allows the implementation of an operation to be changed without affecting the application program that calls it?
questions.explanation: Program-operation independence separates the interface (name/arguments) from the implementation.

questions.text: In a database, a 'Trigger' is defined as:

choices.label: B
choices.text: A rule activated automatically by updates to a table

choices.label: C
choices.text: The primary key of a table

questions.explanation: Triggers are active rules that run automatically when specific data modifications occur.

questions.text: Which type of end user typically writes complex queries to meet new or unusual requirements?
questions.explanation: Sophisticated end users (engineers, scientists, analysts) use the full capabilities of the DBMS for complex tasks.

questions.text: What is the role of 'System Analysts' in the database environment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Writing the code for application programs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Determining the requirements of end users

questions.explanation: System analysts bridge the gap between users and programmers by defining system requirements.

questions.text: Providing backup and recovery is a task of the DBMS intended to protect against:
questions.explanation: The backup and recovery subsystem restores the database to a consistent state after a crash.

questions.text: The conceptual representation of data that hides storage details is known as:
questions.explanation: A data model is a type of data abstraction used to provide a conceptual representation.

questions.text: Which category of database applications is specifically used to control industrial and manufacturing processes?
questions.explanation: Real-time and active database technology is used for process control where time sensitivity is critical.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the main phases of database design mentioned in the slides?
questions.explanation: The standard phases are Requirements, Conceptual, Logical, and Physical design.

questions.text: Which actor is responsible for choosing the structures to represent and store data?
questions.explanation: Database designers identify the data to be stored and choose the structures.

questions.text: What does OLAP stand for in the context of data warehouses?
questions.explanation: OLAP systems extract and analyze business information from very large databases to support decision-making.

questions.text: A 'Key' or 'Uniqueness' constraint ensures that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: All data in the table is numeric

choices.label: B
choices.text: No two records have the same value for a specific field (like Course_number)

choices.label: D
choices.text: The database is shared across multiple servers

questions.explanation: Key constraints ensure each record is uniquely identifiable.

questions.text: Which of the following is a benefit of the database approach regarding software development?
questions.explanation: Using a DBMS provides many standard functions, reducing the time needed to develop custom applications.

questions.text: What is 'Concurrency Control'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Restricting users from logging in after hours

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensuring multiple users can update the same data simultaneously without errors

choices.label: C
choices.text: Running backups while the database is in use

choices.label: D
choices.text: Checking if two records are identical

questions.explanation: Concurrency control software manages simultaneous access to ensure the database remains consistent.

questions.text: Which system uses rules to infer new information from existing facts in the database?
questions.explanation: Deductive database systems provide capabilities for defining deduction rules to infer new information.

questions.text: The transition from the Conceptual design to the Physical design typically includes which intermediary step?
questions.explanation: The workflow is: Requirements -> Conceptual -> Logical -> Physical.

questions.text: In the UNIVERSITY database example, which of these is likely a PREREQUISITE record element?
questions.explanation: Prerequisite records represent the relationships between different courses.

questions.text: Which is NOT a reason to avoid a DBMS?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Simple application not expected to change

questions.explanation: A DBMS actually provides better security via its security and authorization subsystems.

questions.text: Which component is responsible for increasing the efficiency of query processing?
questions.explanation: Indexes are storage structures used to speed up the retrieval of specific data.

questions.text: What type of end users maintain their own personal databases by using ready-made software packages?
questions.explanation: Standalone users maintain personal databases with easy-to-use packages.

questions.text: Complete the logic: Data abstraction = conceptual representation of data + _____
questions.explanation: Data abstraction allows the conceptual representation of data without including details of how it is stored.

questions.text: What is defined as a collection of related data with implicit meaning?
questions.explanation: According to the source, a Database is a collection of related data and known facts that can be recorded and have implicit meaning.

questions.text: The term **Miniworld** or **Universe of Discourse (UoD)** refers to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The hardware used to run the database

choices.label: B
choices.text: The specific aspect of the real world represented in the database

choices.label: C
choices.text: The software catalog used by the DBMS

choices.label: D
choices.text: The collection of all digital information on the internet

questions.explanation: Miniworld or UoD represents some aspect of the real world that is modeled in the database.

questions.text: Which type of database stores images, audio clips, and video streams digitally?
questions.explanation: Multimedia databases are specifically mentioned as storing images, audio, and video streams.

questions.text: Which process involves specifying the data types, structures, and constraints of the data to be stored?
questions.explanation: Defining a database involves specifying data types, structures, and constraints.

questions.text: Descriptive information stored by the DBMS in a catalog or dictionary is known as:
questions.explanation: Meta-data is defined as database definition or descriptive information stored in a catalog.

questions.text: An execution program that includes one or more database accesses is called a(n):
questions.explanation: A transaction is an executing program or process that includes one or more database reads/writes.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a phase of database design mentioned in the text?
questions.explanation: The phases listed are Requirements specification/analysis, Conceptual design, Logical design, and Physical design.

questions.text: In the **UNIVERSITY** database example, which of the following is considered a 'Query' rather than an 'Update'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Change the class of 'Smith' to sophomore

choices.label: B
choices.text: List the prerequisites of the 'Database' course

choices.label: D
choices.text: Create a new section for the 'Database' course

questions.explanation: Listing prerequisites is a retrieval (Query). Changing classes, entering grades, and creating sections are modifications (Updates).

questions.text: What is the main difference between the database approach and traditional file processing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Database approach uses multiple repositories for the same data

choices.label: B
choices.text: File processing maintains a single repository for all users

choices.label: C
choices.text: Database approach provides a single repository where data is defined once and accessed by various users

choices.label: D
choices.text: File processing is more secure than the database approach

questions.explanation: The database approach uses a single repository that maintains data defined once, as opposed to file processing where each user defines their own files.

questions.text: The characteristic that allows the structure of data files to be stored separately from access programs is called:
questions.explanation: Program-data independence refers to the insulation between programs and data, where the data structure is stored in the catalog separately from access programs.

questions.text: Program-operation independence involves dividing an operation into which two parts?
questions.explanation: Operations are specified in two parts: the Interface (name and types) and the Implementation.

questions.text: A **Data Model** is a type of _____ used to provide a conceptual representation of data.
questions.explanation: Data abstraction allows program-data and program-operation independence, and a data model is the tool used to provide this conceptual representation.

questions.text: What is a **View** in a database system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A physical copy of the database for backup

choices.label: B
choices.text: A subset of the database containing virtual data derived from files

choices.label: C
choices.text: The graphical interface used by the DBA

choices.label: D
choices.text: The code used to implement the DBMS

questions.explanation: A View is a subset of the database that contains virtual data derived from database files but not explicitly stored.

questions.text: Which property ensures that either all database operations in a transaction are executed or none are?
questions.explanation: The Atomicity property ensures that all operations in a transaction are executed or none at all.

questions.text: The **Isolation** property of a transaction means:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The transaction results are permanently saved

choices.label: B
choices.text: The transaction must be performed by a single user

choices.label: C
choices.text: Each transaction appears to execute independently from other transactions

choices.label: D
choices.text: The transaction is isolated from the DBMS catalog

questions.explanation: Isolation ensures each transaction appears to execute in isolation from other concurrent transactions.

questions.text: Who is responsible for authorizing access, monitoring use, and acquiring software/hardware resources?
questions.explanation: The DBA is responsible for authorizing access, coordinating use, and managing resources.

questions.text: Which group of end users access the database through canned transactions (e.g., bank tellers)?
questions.explanation: Naive or parametric end users typically use well-defined 'canned' transactions to interact with the database.

questions.text: Which end users maintain personal databases by using ready-made program packages?
questions.explanation: Standalone users maintain their own personal databases using ready-to-use software packages.

questions.text: Who determines the requirements of end users and develops specifications for canned transactions?
questions.explanation: System analysts determine the requirements of end users.

questions.text: Personnel responsible for running and maintaining the hardware and software environment for the database system are called:
questions.explanation: Operators and maintenance personnel run and maintain the hardware and software environment.

questions.text: What is **controlled redundancy** primarily used for in the DBMS approach?
questions.explanation: Controlled redundancy (denormalization) is sometimes necessary to improve query performance.

questions.text: The **impedance mismatch problem** refers to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hardware incompatibility between CPU and Storage

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mismatch between the data structures of a programming language and the DBMS storage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conflicts between multiple users accessing the same record

questions.explanation: Impedance mismatch refers to the difficulty in providing persistent storage for program objects (like C++ objects) because of structural differences between the language and the DB.

questions.text: Which of the following is an integrity constraint that requires every section record to be related to a course record?
questions.explanation: Referential integrity ensures relationships between records (e.g., section must relate to a course).

questions.text: A rule activated by updates to a table is known as a:
questions.explanation: A Trigger is a rule activated by updates to a table.

questions.text: What type of system allows for defining deduction rules to infer new information from stored facts?
questions.explanation: Deductive database systems provide capabilities for defining rules to infer new information.

questions.text: In which scenario is it **NOT** desirable to use a DBMS?

choices.label: A
choices.text: When multiple users need simultaneous access

choices.label: B
choices.text: When data requirements are complex and likely to change

choices.label: C
choices.text: When there are stringent, real-time requirements that DBMS overhead may hinder

choices.label: D
choices.text: When data needs to be integrated across different departments

questions.explanation: The text states not to use a DBMS if stringent real-time requirements cannot be met due to DBMS overhead.

questions.text: The property that allows multiple users to update the same data in a controlled manner is managed by:
questions.explanation: Concurrency control software ensures that multiple users updating the same data do so correctly.

questions.text: Which characteristic of the database approach refers to the 'Self-describing nature'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Programs can access data without a catalog

choices.label: B
choices.text: The system contains a complete definition of the structure and constraints (Meta-data)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Users must define the data structure in their code

choices.label: D
choices.text: The database contains its own backup files

questions.explanation: The self-describing nature means the system contains the meta-data (definitions/constraints) in a catalog.

questions.text: Data normalization is a technique used to:

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provide faster query response regardless of space

questions.explanation: The text lists 'Controlling redundancy' through 'Data normalization' as an advantage.

questions.text: Which type of user uses the DBMS catalog to find information about the database structure?
questions.explanation: The database catalog is used by both the DBMS software and database users who need info on structure.

questions.text: Which technology is typically used to control industrial and manufacturing processes?
questions.explanation: Real-time and active database technology is used for industrial and manufacturing control.

questions.text: Database maintenance involves:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Preventing any changes to the database structure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Allowing the system to evolve as requirements change

choices.label: D
choices.text: Writing application code for the user

questions.explanation: Maintaining the database involves allowing the system to evolve as requirements change over time.

questions.text: What is the primary responsibility of a **Database Designer**?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identifying data to be stored and choosing structures to represent it

choices.label: D
choices.text: Writing user manuals for the database

questions.explanation: Designers identify data and choose appropriate structures.

questions.text: Which category of end users includes engineers and scientists who thoroughly learn the DBMS facilities?
questions.explanation: Sophisticated end users (engineers, scientists) learn the DBMS thoroughly to implement complex applications.

questions.text: Which of these is **NOT** a reason to avoid using a DBMS?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The database application is simple and unlikely to change

choices.label: C
choices.text: The data needs to be accessed by multiple users simultaneously

questions.explanation: If multiple users need access, a DBMS is actually preferred. The other options are reasons mentioned in the text for NOT using a DBMS.

questions.text: What does a **Key or Uniqueness constraint** ensure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: That the database is locked to only one user

choices.label: B
choices.text: That every record in a file has a unique value for a specific element (like Course_number)

choices.label: C
choices.text: That only the DBA has the key to the server room

choices.label: D
choices.text: That data is encrypted with a unique key

questions.explanation: A key/uniqueness constraint ensures every record has a unique value for a specific data element.

questions.text: Which of the following describes **Casual end users**?

choices.label: B
choices.text: They occasionally access the database but may need different information each time

choices.label: D
choices.text: They maintain personal databases on their PCs

questions.explanation: Casual end users occasionally access the database, needing different info each time.

questions.text: Which of the following is an implication of using the database approach?
questions.explanation: Reduced development time, flexibility, availability of up-to-date info, and economies of scale are listed as implications.
